topic: A person accidentally sends a text to the wrong recipient
raw_output:
[story 1]

My thumb hovered over the screen, a tight knot forming in my chest. I had intended to send a perfectly calibrated, mildly sarcastic reply to my colleague about a shared project deadline. Instead, my finger slipped, and the message shot out, landing squarely in the inbox of my boss’s estranged ex-girlfriend. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, turning my face a deep crimson. I stared at the phone as if it were a venomous snake. Every nerve ending felt coiled, vibrating with a sharp, unwelcome tension. I slammed the phone onto the desk, the plastic rattling loudly. "Oh, for crying out loud," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I couldn't believe the sheer, monumental idiocy of the mistake.

[story 2]

The notification popped up, and the blood drained from my face. It was a text meant for my sister, a casual, silly meme. My mistake, a misplaced number, a momentary lapse in concentration, and now that message was broadcasting to a complete stranger. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscles twitching beneath my skin. I snatched the phone up, my hands shaking violently, and frantically started typing a retraction, a desperate plea to undo the damage. The silence in the room amplified the frantic pounding in my ears. I paced three steps toward the window, my breath coming in ragged gasps, every muscle rigid with sheer frustration at the catastrophic error.

[story 3]

I reread the text twice. It was a simple greeting, utterly innocuous, yet the fact that it had gone to the wrong person sent a wave of intense agitation through me. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the table. I could feel a pressure building behind my eyes, a hot, prickly sensation that made my vision narrow slightly. I took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to force the frantic energy down, but it only seemed to fuel the internal storm. I started pacing the small kitchen, my movements sharp and jerky, my gaze fixed on the phone screen as if it held the key to undoing the entire, awful sequence of events. I wanted to scream, to shatter something, but all that emerged was a strained, low hiss of pure vexation.
